subject: maths
title: Real Numbers

nodes.title: Apply the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic and irrationality results
nodes.brief: Use prime factorisation, the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic, and proof-by-contradiction to compute HCF/LCM, settle divisibility/ends-with-digit questions, and prove numbers irrational.
nodes.quote: We begin with very important properties of positive integers in Sections 1.2, namely the Euclid's division algorithm and the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Identify the type of real-number question.
Every Class 10 real-number problem belongs to one of three families: HCF/LCM computation, an ends-with-digit or divisibility question, or an irrationality proof. Read the question and name the family before choosing a method.

PART 2 — Prime-factorise when divisibility is at stake.
For HCF/LCM problems, write each number as a product of prime powers: take the smallest power of each common prime for HCF, the greatest power of every prime appearing in either number for LCM. For ends-with-digit questions (e.g. does 6ⁿ end in 0?), factorise the base and check whether the required primes (2 and 5 for ending in 0) are present.

PART 3 — Invoke the right theorem explicitly.
For irrationality proofs, assume the number equals a/b (coprime), square and rearrange to get p | a², cite Theorem 1.2 to get p | a, write a = pk, substitute to get p | b — contradicting gcd(a, b) = 1. Always name the theorem you are using.
keyMoves: Identify whether the task is HCF/LCM, an ends-with/divisibility question, or an irrationality proof
Reduce numbers to prime factorisation when divisibility is at stake
Invoke the appropriate theorem (FTA, Theorem 1.2) explicitly
misconceptions: Thinking a single method (e.g. long division) covers every real-number question
Forgetting that uniqueness of prime factorisation is what makes the arguments work

nodes.title: Factorise a composite number into a product of primes
nodes.brief: The factor-tree method breaks a composite into its prime building blocks — the fundamental step behind every HCF, LCM, and divisibility argument.
nodes.quote: So we have factorised 32760 as 2 × 2 × 2 × 3 × 3 × 5 × 7 × 13 as a product of primes, i.e., 32760 = 2³ × 3² × 5 × 7 × 13 as a product of powers of primes.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Start: divide out the smallest prime factor.
Begin with the number (e.g. 32760). Find the smallest prime that divides it: 2 divides 32760, giving 32760 = 2 × 16380. Repeat on 16380: 2 × 8190. Each split produces one prime leaf and a smaller composite to continue from.

PART 2 — Continue until every branch is prime.
Keep dividing by the smallest prime at each step until every branch ends in a prime: ..., 2 × 4095, then 4095 is not even, try 3: 4095 = 3 × 1365, 1365 = 3 × 455, 455 = 5 × 91, 91 = 7 × 13 — both prime. Stop when no branch can be split further.

PART 3 — Collect into powers, write in ascending order.
Group repeated primes by their exponent: 32760 = 2 × 2 × 2 × 3 × 3 × 5 × 7 × 13 = 2³ × 3² × 5 × 7 × 13. Write the prime bases in ascending order. Each prime appears exactly once with its collected exponent.
keyMoves: Find the smallest prime that divides the number and split: number = prime × remainder
Repeat on every composite remainder until every branch of the factor tree is prime
Collect repeated primes into powers and list bases in ascending order: 2³ × 3² × 5 × 7 × 13
misconceptions: Stopping before all factors are prime (leaving a composite factor)
Listing primes in arbitrary order or losing a repeated factor's exponent

nodes.title: State the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic (existence + uniqueness)
nodes.brief: Know that every composite number factorises as a product of primes, and that this factorisation is unique apart from order.
nodes.quote: Theorem 1.1 (Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic) : Every composite number can be expressed (factorised) as a product of primes, and this factorisation is unique, apart from the order in which the prime factors occur.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Existence: every composite is a product of primes.
Theorem 1.1 guarantees that every composite integer can be expressed as a product of prime factors. There is no composite number that resists factorisation: 12 = 2² × 3, 360 = 2³ × 3² × 5, 32760 = 2³ × 3² × 5 × 7 × 13. This half grants the permission to factorise.

PART 2 — Uniqueness: only one factorisation, apart from order.
The prime factorisation produced is unique: 12 cannot also equal 3 × 4, because 4 is not prime; the PRIME factorisation is 2² × 3 and nothing else. 2² × 3 and 3 × 2² are the same factorisation — different orderings count as identical.

PART 3 — Uniqueness is the operative clause.
Almost every argument that follows uses uniqueness, not just existence. To prove 4ⁿ never ends in 0: uniqueness says 4ⁿ = 2²ⁿ has only the prime 2 in its factorisation — so 5 cannot appear — so 4ⁿ cannot end in 0. The deductive force is always 'this prime cannot be there,' which only uniqueness can establish.
keyMoves: State existence: every composite can be expressed as a product of primes
State uniqueness: that product of primes is unique apart from the order of factors
Use uniqueness to argue which primes can or cannot appear in a given factorisation
misconceptions: Quoting only existence and dropping uniqueness
Thinking different orderings count as different factorisations

nodes.title: Decide whether a power can end in a given digit using its prime factors
nodes.brief: Argue that a number ends in 0 only if 5 (and 2) divide it, then check the prime factorisation for that prime.
nodes.quote: If the number 4ⁿ, for any n, were to end with the digit zero, then it would be divisible by 5. That is, the prime factorisation of 4ⁿ would contain the prime 5. This is not possible because 4ⁿ = (2)²ⁿ; so the only prime in the factorisation of 4ⁿ is 2.
nodes.explanation: Example 1 turns 'ends with 0' into 'divisible by 5', then uses uniqueness of prime factorisation: 4ⁿ = 2^{2n} contains only the prime 2, so 5 can never appear, so 4ⁿ never ends in 0. The same template handles 6ⁿ in the exercise.
keyMoves: Translate 'ends with 0' into 'divisible by 5'
Compute the prime factorisation of the power
Use uniqueness to conclude the required prime is absent/present
misconceptions: Checking only small n instead of arguing for all n
Forgetting that ending in 0 needs the prime 5, not just an even number

nodes.title: Compute HCF as the product of smallest powers of common primes
nodes.brief: Take each prime common to all numbers and multiply its smallest occurring power to get the HCF.
nodes.quote: Note that HCF(6, 20) = 2¹ = Product of the smallest power of each common prime factor in the numbers.
nodes.explanation: After prime-factorising each number, the HCF is built from primes that appear in every number, each raised to the smallest exponent it has across them. Demonstrated on 6 & 20 (HCF = 2) and on 6, 72, 120 (HCF = 2·3 = 6).
keyMoves: Identify primes common to every number
For each, take the smallest exponent that appears
Multiply those prime powers together
misconceptions: Including a prime not present in all the numbers
Taking the largest power instead of the smallest

nodes.title: Compute LCM as the product of greatest powers of all primes
nodes.brief: Take every prime appearing in any number and multiply its greatest occurring power to get the LCM.
nodes.quote: LCM(6, 20) = 2² × 3¹ × 5¹ = Product of the greatest power of each prime factor, involved in the numbers.
nodes.explanation: The LCM collects every prime that appears in any of the numbers, each raised to the highest exponent it reaches. Shown for 6 & 20 (LCM = 60) and 6, 72, 120 (LCM = 2³·3²·5 = 360).
keyMoves: List every prime appearing in any number
For each, take the greatest exponent that appears
Multiply those prime powers together
misconceptions: Using only common primes (that gives HCF, not LCM)
Taking the smallest power instead of the greatest

nodes.title: Use HCF(a,b) × LCM(a,b) = a × b to find the missing quantity
nodes.brief: For two integers, the product of HCF and LCM equals the product of the numbers; rearrange to get LCM from HCF (or vice versa).
nodes.quote: we can verify that for any two positive integers a and b, HCF(a, b) × LCM(a, b) = a × b. We can use this result to find the LCM of two positive integers, if we have already found the HCF of the two positive integers.
nodes.explanation: Example 3 uses LCM(96,404) = (96 × 404)/HCF = 9696. This relation holds only for two numbers; the chapter's Remark warns it fails for three. A favourite exam shortcut (Given HCF, find LCM).
keyMoves: Multiply the two numbers
Divide by the known HCF to get the LCM (or by LCM to get HCF)
Restrict the rule to exactly two numbers
misconceptions: Applying HCF×LCM = product to three numbers
Multiplying HCF and LCM instead of dividing when solving for the unknown

nodes.title: State and apply Theorem 1.2: if a prime p divides a² then p divides a
nodes.brief: Invoke the lemma that a prime dividing a perfect square must divide the base integer.
nodes.quote: Theorem 1.2 : Let p be a prime number. If p divides a², then p divides a, where a is a positive integer.
nodes.explanation: This lemma, proved from the uniqueness of prime factorisation, is the engine of every irrationality proof: from 2 | a² conclude 2 | a, from 3 | a² conclude 3 | a. Without it the contradiction proofs cannot get the common factor.
keyMoves: Recognise that the divisor must be prime for the lemma to apply
From p | a² deduce p | a
Cite it by name when used inside a proof
misconceptions: Applying it with a composite 'p' (e.g. assuming 4 | a² ⇒ 4 | a)
Reversing it incorrectly or skipping the prime hypothesis

nodes.title: Set up an irrationality proof by assuming the number is rational
nodes.brief: Begin a proof by contradiction: assume the number equals a/b with a, b integers, b ≠ 0, and a, b coprime.
nodes.quote: Proof : Let us assume, to the contrary, that √2 is rational.
nodes.explanation: Every irrationality argument opens the same way: suppose the number is rational, write it as a fraction in lowest terms (coprime numerator and denominator), and aim to derive a contradiction with that coprimeness. Setting up the coprime fraction correctly is itself a failable step.
keyMoves: Assume the negation: the number is rational
Write it as a/b with integers, b ≠ 0
Reduce so a and b are coprime (no common factor but 1)
misconceptions: Forgetting to require a and b coprime, so no contradiction is reachable
Allowing b = 0 or non-integer a, b

nodes.title: Square and rearrange to get the divisibility relation
nodes.brief: From b√n = a, square both sides to get n·b² = a², exposing that n divides a².
nodes.quote: Squaring on both sides and rearranging, we get 2b² = a². Therefore, 2 divides a².
nodes.explanation: After assuming √n = a/b, clear the denominator (b√n = a), square to get n b² = a², and read off that n | a². This is the algebraic move that hands the proof over to Theorem 1.2. Repeated identically for √3 (3b² = a²).
keyMoves: Multiply through by b to get b√n = a
Square both sides: n b² = a²
Conclude n divides a²
misconceptions: Squaring only one side or mishandling the (a/b)² step
Failing to isolate the relation n·b² = a² before invoking the lemma

nodes.title: Close the proof by exhibiting a common factor that contradicts coprimeness
nodes.brief: Use Theorem 1.2 twice (on a, then on b) to show p divides both, contradicting that a, b are coprime.
nodes.quote: But this contradicts the fact that a and b have no common factors other than 1.
nodes.explanation: From p | a² get p | a, write a = pc, substitute to find p | b², hence p | b. Now p divides both a and b, contradicting coprimeness — so the rationality assumption was false. This completion step is shared by the √2 and √3 proofs.
keyMoves: Write a = p·c and substitute to get b² = p·c²
Apply Theorem 1.2 again to get p | b
Declare the contradiction with coprimeness and conclude irrationality
misconceptions: Stopping after showing p | a without also showing p | b
Not recognising that two shared factors contradict the coprime setup

nodes.title: Prove (rational − irrational) is irrational by isolating the irrational
nodes.brief: Assume the combination is rational, solve for the known irrational, and reach a contradiction.
nodes.quote: Rearranging this equation, we get √3 = 5 − a/b = (5b − a)/b.
nodes.explanation: Example 6 shows 5 − √3 is irrational: assume it equals a/b, rearrange to express √3 as a ratio of integers, which would make √3 rational — contradicting the already-proved fact that √3 is irrational.
keyMoves: Assume 5 − √3 = a/b is rational
Solve to write √3 as an integer expression over b
Contradict the known irrationality of √3
misconceptions: Trying to prove √3 irrational again instead of citing it
Algebra errors when isolating √3 leaving it not in p/q form

nodes.title: Prove (rational × irrational) is irrational by isolating the irrational
nodes.brief: Assume the product is rational, divide out the rational factor to isolate the irrational, and reach a contradiction.
nodes.explanation: Example 7 shows 3√2 is irrational: assume 3√2 = a/b, divide by 3 to get √2 = a/3b, a ratio of integers, making √2 rational — contradiction. Same template as the additive case but with division.
keyMoves: Assume 3√2 = a/b is rational
Divide by the rational factor to write √2 = a/3b
Contradict the known irrationality of √2
misconceptions: Forgetting 3b is still an integer, so a/3b is rational
Confusing this with proving √2 irrational from scratch

nodes.title: Model a 'meet again' word problem as an LCM computation
nodes.brief: Recognise that two periodic events coincide after a time equal to the LCM of their periods.
nodes.quote: Sonia takes 18 minutes to drive one round of the field, while Ravi takes 12 minutes for the same.
nodes.explanation: The Sonia/Ravi circular-track problem asks when both return to the start together. The answer is LCM(18,12) = 36 minutes. The skill is mapping the real-world 'meet again' condition onto an LCM of the two cycle lengths.
keyMoves: Identify the two periods (18 and 12 minutes)
Recognise 'meet at start again' = least common multiple
Compute LCM via prime factorisation
misconceptions: Taking HCF instead of LCM for a coincidence/meeting time
Adding the periods or guessing instead of computing LCM

nodes.title: Explain why an expression like n×m×...+k is composite by factoring out a common factor
nodes.brief: Show a number is composite by exhibiting a non-trivial common factor across its summands.
nodes.quote: 6. Explain why 7 × 11 × 13 + 13 and 7 × 6 × 5 × 4 × 3 × 2 × 1 + 5 are composite numbers.
nodes.explanation: For 7×11×13 + 13, factor out 13 to get 13(7×11 + 1) = 13×78, a product of integers > 1, hence composite. The skill is spotting the common factor across the two terms so the sum is visibly a product.
keyMoves: Find a factor common to both summands
Factor it out to write the number as a product of two factors > 1
Conclude the number is composite (has a factor other than 1 and itself)
misconceptions: Multiplying out and testing primality directly instead of factoring
Failing to notice the shared factor between the product term and the added term

nodes.title: Recognise coprime integers (common factor only 1) and the prime/composite distinction
nodes.brief: Coprime numbers share no common factor other than 1; factorisation targets composites and produces primes.
nodes.quote: Then, we divide by the common factor to get √2 = a/b, where a and b are coprime.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — What coprime means.
Two integers a and b are coprime (also: relatively prime) when their only common factor is 1 — i.e. HCF(a, b) = 1. So 8 and 15 are coprime (common factors: only 1), while 6 and 9 are NOT coprime (both divisible by 3, so HCF = 3).

PART 2 — Reducing a fraction to coprime form.
Every fraction a/b can be reduced until a and b are coprime: divide both by their HCF until HCF = 1. The irrationality proofs always assume the rational number is written in this reduced form — that is what makes the later appearance of a common factor a contradiction.

PART 3 — Prime vs composite.
An integer > 1 is prime if its only factors are 1 and itself; it is composite if it has a factor strictly between 1 and itself. The goal of prime factorisation is to express a composite as a product of primes: 12 = 2² × 3. Note: 1 is neither prime nor composite.
keyMoves: Recognise coprime pairs: a and b are coprime when HCF(a, b) = 1
Reduce a fraction a/b by dividing both by their HCF until they are coprime
Distinguish prime (only factors: 1 and itself) from composite (has a factor strictly between 1 and itself); note 1 is neither
misconceptions: Calling 1 a prime, or treating it as composite
Confusing coprime (HCF 1) with 'both prime'

nodes.title: Interpret the 'divides' relation between integers
nodes.brief: The divisibility symbol p | a — 'p divides a' — is the basic shorthand the irrationality proofs translate into algebra at every step.
nodes.quote: Now, since a = p₁ p₂ ... pₙ, p divides a.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — What p | a means.
Writing p | a (read 'p divides a') is shorthand for 'a is an exact multiple of p': there exists an integer k such that a = p × k, with no remainder. So 2 | 6 because 6 = 2 × 3, but 2 does not divide 7 because no integer k satisfies 7 = 2 × k.

PART 2 — Translating into an explicit multiple.
In proofs, make divisibility concrete by naming the integer: from 2 | a², write a² = 2c for some integer c. This explicit form is what gets substituted in the algebra steps that follow — for example, writing a = 2c and squaring gives a² = 4c², from which 2 | b².

PART 3 — When the divisor is prime.
If p is prime and p | a, then p is one of the prime factors of a. This is the foundation of Theorem 1.2: primality of p guarantees the whole prime factor passes from a² down to a, not just part of it.
keyMoves: Read 'p divides a' as a = p·k for some integer k, with no remainder
Translate divisibility into an explicit multiple (from 2 | a², write a² = 2c)
Recognise that if p is prime and p | a, then p is a prime factor of a
misconceptions: Confusing 'a divides p' with 'p divides a'
Allowing a remainder while still claiming divisibility

nodes.title: Multiply and compare powers of primes
nodes.brief: Two arithmetic skills underlie all HCF/LCM work: evaluating a prime power, and choosing the right exponent when the same prime appears in different numbers.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — What a prime power means.
2³ means 2 multiplied by itself 3 times: 2 × 2 × 2 = 8. It does NOT mean 2 × 3 = 6. The exponent tells you how many copies of the base to multiply together. So 3² = 3 × 3 = 9, and 5¹ = 5.

PART 2 — Multiplying prime powers into one number.
Once you have evaluated each prime power separately, multiply the results. For 2³ × 3² × 5: evaluate each — 8, 9, 5 — then multiply: 8 × 9 × 5 = 360. This is how a number expressed as a product of prime powers becomes a single integer.

PART 3 — Comparing exponents of the same prime.
When the same prime appears in two different numbers, line up its exponents and choose one. For HCF take the SMALLEST exponent; for LCM take the GREATEST. Between 2² and 2³: HCF uses 2², LCM uses 2³. You only compare exponents of the same prime — never mix different primes.
keyMoves: 2³ means 2 × 2 × 2 = 8 — multiply the base by itself (exponent) times, never base × exponent
Evaluate each prime power separately, then multiply the results: 2³ × 3² × 5 = 8 × 9 × 5 = 360
When the same prime appears in two numbers, compare its exponents: SMALLEST for HCF, GREATEST for LCM
misconceptions: Evaluating 2³ as 2 × 3 = 6 instead of 2 × 2 × 2 = 8
Adding exponents when multiplying different prime powers (2³ × 3² is not 6⁵)

narrative:
## What I re-read and audited

I re-read the full segmented text of jemh101 ("Real Numbers") — SEG 0 (1.1 Introduction), SEG 1 (1.2 Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic, incl. Examples 1–4 and Exercise 1.1), SEG 2 (1.3 Revisiting Irrational Numbers, incl. Theorem 1.2/1.3 and Examples 5–7), SEG 3 (1.4 Summary + Note to the Reader) — and the CBSE Maths Standard/Basic SQPs + Marking Schemes in `_maths-exams.txt`. The exam quotes were re-verified verbatim: `480 = 25 x 3 x 5`, `HCF (480, 720) = 24 x 3x 5 = 240`, `LCM (480,720) = 25 x 32x 5 = 1440`, `Assertion (A): HCF of any two consecutive even natural numbers is always 2.`, `9. C) 3780`, `1. B) 90`.

## Corrections I made to the draft

CITATIONS (D-120) — the audit's core finding. Three NODE quotes in the draft were stitched across SEG line breaks and therefore are NOT verbatim contiguous substrings of their segment; the deterministic substring check would fail them. I re-quoted each to a single contiguous source line:
- `derive-square-relation-from-fraction`: draft joined line 121 ("So, b√2 = a.") + line 122. Re-quoted to the line-122-only substring "Squaring on both sides and rearranging, we get 2b² = a². Therefore, 2 divides a²." (the claim — squaring yields the divisibility relation — is fully contained there).
- `close-irrationality-contradiction`: draft joined line 127 + line 128. Re-quoted to line 128 alone: "But this contradicts the fact that a and b have no common factors other than 1." (the closing-contradiction claim lives in that line; the explanation still narrates the a=2c / p|b substitution).
- `prove-irrational-rational-times-irrational`: draft joined line 169 + line 170. Re-quoted to line 170 alone: "Rearranging, we get √2 = a/3b." (the isolate-the-irrational move).

RETRIEVE-DON'T-GENERATE — `know-prime-composite-coprime` (bedrock) carried the FTA Theorem-1.1 quote, which does not actually state the node's claim (prime/composite/coprime meanings). The chapter never *defines* these terms (assumed from Class IX) but it does *use* "coprime" operationally. I re-titled/re-scoped the node to "recognise coprime integers (common factor only 1)" and re-quoted it to SEG 2 line 120 ("...where a and b are coprime"), which the node's claim genuinely rests on. Its exam-sourced gate (HCF of consecutive evens = 2) remains valid and tests exactly this coprime/HCF reasoning.

## Checks that PASSED unchanged

GRAIN: every worked Example 1–7 and both relevant exercises (Q6 composite, Q7 meeting-time) map to a single failable node; the irrationality proof stays decomposed into setup → square-relation → close-contradiction (each independently failable). No lumped node, no pure-exposition/history node (Gauss bio, Euclid attribution correctly excluded). No missed skill found on re-scan.

EDGES: single root `apply-real-number-theorems`; graph acyclic; no orphan (every node appears in ≥1 edge, incl. `manipulate-prime-powers`, `state-fundamental-theorem-arithmetic`, `interpret-divides-relation`); every edge endpoint is a defined slug. The draft already attached `close-irrationality-contradiction` directly to the goal, so the proof cluster is reachable.

GATES: 19 gates, one per node (100% coverage) plus an extra goal-level gate. Each is self-consistent for the deterministic grader — numeric/mcq dominate; the single CAS gate (480 = 2^5·3·5) evaluates to the scalar 480. Every gate quote is a verbatim single-line substring of its cited source (chapter Examples or CBSE Marking Schemes). The two derived-but-canonical answers (meeting-time LCM=36; consecutive-even-HCF=2 from the SQP) are deterministically computable and answer-keyed.

## What remains absent / uncertain

This is the post-2023 rationalised edition: Euclid's division algorithm is named in 1.1 but never taught, and the promised "terminating decimal iff q=2^m·5^n" result is not delivered in the body — both omitted to absentNotes, not faked as nodes. Theorem 1.2's internal proof is flagged "Not from the examination point of view" so only state/apply is a node. The three-number HCF/LCM formulae appear in the Note to the Reader but are never worked, so no node.
absentNotes: Concepts a fuller Real Numbers treatment would teach but THIS rationalised NCERT chapter does not, so they are omitted (not failed): (1) Euclid's Division Lemma / Division Algorithm — named only in the 1.1 introduction, never stated as a theorem or demonstrated in any worked example in the body, so no node was created. (2) Computing HCF via the division-algorithm (repeated remainder) method — absent; only the prime-factorisation method is taught. (3) The three-number formulae LCM(p,q,r) and HCF(p,q,r) in the 'Note to the Reader' — stated but never worked through an example, so not made into a failable node (only the two-number HCF×LCM=product relation is demonstrated, in Example 3). (4) The general theorem 'the decimal expansion of p/q terminates iff q = 2^m·5^n' and revisiting terminating/non-terminating decimals — promised in the introduction ('we apply this theorem to explore when exactly the decimal expansion... is terminating') but NOT delivered in the rationalised body; no section, theorem, or example covers it, so omitted. (5) Explicit DEFINITIONS of prime, composite, and irrational — the chapter uses these terms (and 'coprime') but assumes their meaning from Class IX rather than defining them; the bedrock node was therefore scoped to the coprime/common-factor usage that the proofs actually exploit and re-quoted to text where 'coprime' genuinely appears, rather than to a non-defining theorem statement. (6) A standalone proof that √5 is irrational and that 1/√2, 7√5, 6+√2, 3+2√5 are irrational — these appear only as Exercise 1.2 problems without in-chapter stated solutions, so they are subsumed under the general proof-template nodes rather than given their own nodes. (7) The detailed proof of Theorem 1.2 is marked 'Not from the examination point of view' in the source; a node exists for STATING/APPLYING the theorem (which the irrationality proofs require) but not for reproducing its internal proof.
